name: Grain-Free
description: Grain-free diets replace traditional grains with alternative carbohydrate sources like sweet potatoes, peas, and lentils. Popular for dogs with grain sensitivities.
benefits: May reduce allergic reactions
Often higher in protein
Can improve coat quality
risks: Potential link to DCM in some breeds
May be unnecessary for most dogs
Often more expensive

name: Raw Food (BARF)
description: Raw food diets, often called BARF (Biologically Appropriate Raw Food), consist of uncooked meat, bones, organs, and vegetables. Proponents argue it mimics a dog's ancestral diet.
suitable_for: owners committed to food safety practices
benefits: Improved coat and skin condition
Smaller and firmer stools
Higher moisture content
Potential dental benefits from raw bones
risks: Risk of bacterial contamination (Salmonella, Listeria)
Nutritional imbalance if not properly formulated
Potential for bone splintering
Higher cost and preparation time

name: Senior Formula
description: Senior diets are specially formulated for dogs aged 7 and older, typically featuring reduced calories, adjusted protein levels, and added joint-supporting nutrients like glucosamine and chondroitin.
benefits: Supports joint health with added glucosamine
Adjusted calorie density for less active dogs
Often includes antioxidants for cognitive support
May support kidney function
risks: Not all senior dogs need reduced protein
Blanket formulas may not suit all older dogs
Some seniors need more calories than senior food provides

name: Puppy Formula
description: Puppy diets provide the higher calorie density, protein, calcium, and DHA that growing dogs need for bone development, brain growth, and immune system support. Formulated for dogs under 12 months (or up to 24 months for large breeds).
benefits: Higher DHA for brain and eye development
Optimal calcium-to-phosphorus ratio for bone growth
Calorie-dense to support rapid growth
Immune-supporting nutrients
risks: Overfeeding can cause rapid growth in large breeds leading to joint problems
Not suitable for adult dogs (excess calcium)
Large breed puppies need specific large-breed puppy food

name: Weight Management
description: Weight management diets are calorie-restricted formulas designed to help overweight dogs lose weight or maintain a healthy weight. They typically have higher fiber content to promote satiety and lower fat levels.
suitable_for: neutered dogs prone to weight gain
benefits: Reduced calorie density to support weight loss
Higher fiber keeps dogs feeling full
Reduces strain on joints from excess weight
Lowers risk of diabetes and heart disease
risks: May lack sufficient nutrients if fed incorrectly
Not suitable for puppies or pregnant dogs
Some dogs may lose muscle mass if protein is too low

name: High-Protein
description: High-protein diets contain elevated levels of animal-based proteins, typically 30% or more on a dry matter basis. Designed for active, working, or athletic dogs with high energy demands.
suitable_for: dogs recovering from illness or surgery
benefits: Supports lean muscle mass
Provides sustained energy for active dogs
Can aid muscle recovery after exercise
Often highly palatable
risks: May worsen kidney disease in dogs with pre-existing conditions
Not appropriate for sedentary or overweight dogs
Can cause loose stools if introduced too quickly

name: Limited Ingredient
description: Limited ingredient diets (LID) are formulated with a minimal number of protein and carbohydrate sources, making it easier to identify and eliminate food allergens. Typically feature a single novel protein and one carbohydrate source.
suitable_for: dogs with food allergies or intolerances
dogs with chronic itching or hot spots
benefits: Simplifies allergy identification
Reduces chance of triggering food sensitivities
Often uses novel proteins dogs haven't been exposed to
Good for dogs with multiple ingredient sensitivities
risks: May not be nutritionally complete if poorly formulated
Novel proteins can become sensitising over time
More expensive than standard diets
May not resolve non-food allergies

name: Wet Food (Canned)
description: Wet or canned food contains 70-80% moisture and is often more palatable than dry kibble. It can be fed as a complete diet or used as a topper. Suitable for dogs of all life stages depending on the specific formula.
suitable_for: dogs with kidney disease needing increased hydration
benefits: High moisture content supports hydration and urinary health
Highly palatable for fussy dogs
Lower carbohydrate content than many kibbles
Easier to eat for dogs with dental issues
risks: More expensive per calorie than dry food
Can contribute to dental plaque if used exclusively
Shorter shelf life once opened
Some formulas are nutritionally incomplete

name: Dry Kibble
description: Dry kibble is the most widely fed dog food format, offering convenience, long shelf life, and dental benefits from the crunching action. It comes in formulas for every life stage and breed size.
suitable_for: dogs without swallowing or dental difficulties
benefits: Convenient and easy to store
Generally cost-effective
Wide range of formulas available
Crunching texture may help reduce tartar
risks: Lower moisture content may not suit all dogs
Quality varies widely between brands
Some formulas high in fillers and low in meat content
Highly processed compared to fresh diets

name: Homemade
description: Homemade diets are prepared from scratch by owners using whole food ingredients. While they offer full control over ingredients, they require careful formulation to ensure nutritional completeness and balance.
suitable_for: owners with time and nutritional knowledge
dogs whose conditions require highly customised nutrition
dogs with poor response to commercial foods
benefits: Full control over ingredient quality and sourcing
Avoids artificial additives and preservatives
Can be tailored exactly to a dog's health needs
Fresh, minimally processed ingredients
risks: Nutritional imbalance is common without expert guidance
Time-consuming to prepare properly
Risk of bacterial contamination if handling is poor
Requires consultation with a veterinary nutritionist
recommended_brands: N/A — consult a board-certified veterinary nutritionist
Use BalanceIT or PetDiets.com for recipe formulation

small.cups_per_day: Calculated by vet nutritionist based on recipe

medium.cups_per_day: Calculated by vet nutritionist based on recipe

large.cups_per_day: Calculated by vet nutritionist based on recipe

giant.cups_per_day: Calculated by vet nutritionist based on recipe

name: Prescription / Veterinary Diet
description: Prescription diets are clinically formulated to manage specific medical conditions including kidney disease, bladder stones, liver disease, gastrointestinal disorders, diabetes, and food allergies. Available only through a veterinarian.
suitable_for: dogs with urinary tract issues or bladder stones
dogs with pancreatitis or liver disease
dogs with IBD or severe food allergies
benefits: Clinically proven to support management of specific conditions
Precisely formulated nutrient profiles for therapeutic use
Backed by veterinary research and trials
Can significantly improve quality and length of life
risks: Requires ongoing veterinary supervision
More expensive than standard commercial diets
Dogs may refuse the food due to taste restrictions
Not suitable for healthy dogs without a diagnosis

name: Breed-Specific
description: Breed-specific diets are tailored to the unique physiological, metabolic, and health predispositions of particular breeds. Kibble shape, size, nutrient ratios, and functional ingredients are customised for each breed's known needs.
suitable_for: purebred dogs of the target breed
breeds with specific jaw structure or chewing styles
breeds predisposed to certain health conditions
benefits: Kibble size and shape optimised for breed's jaw and eating style
Nutrient profile addresses breed-specific health risks
Palatability often optimised for breed preferences
Targeted functional ingredients (e.g., cardiac support for Boxers)
risks: More expensive than universal formulas
Nutritional differences from standard formulas are often minor
Not always necessary for every individual dog of that breed
Mixed-breed dogs may not benefit

small.cups_per_day: Per packaging for specific breed formula

medium.cups_per_day: Per packaging for specific breed formula

large.cups_per_day: Per packaging for specific breed formula

giant.cups_per_day: Per packaging for specific breed formula
